timeline.description: Start gathering information and reflecting on what you want.

tasks.text: Talk to your Grade 8 teacher about your academic strengths and interests
tasks.tip: Ask for an honest assessment — teachers often see talents you don't notice in yourself.

tasks.text: Find your designated home school using the TDSB School Locator (tdsb.on.ca) or TCDSB website (tcdsb.org)
tasks.tip: Enter your home address on your board's website to confirm which high school you're assigned to.

tasks.text: Start a list of interests and passions — what subjects or activities energize you?
tasks.tip: Include things outside school too: hobbies, sports, creative pursuits.

tasks.text: Ask a parent or guardian to explore the TDSB or TCDSB specialized programs page
tasks.tip: TDSB: tdsb.on.ca → Programs. TCDSB: tcdsb.org → Programs. Both boards publish their full list of specialized programs and application timelines.

tasks.text: If interested in gifted programs, speak to your principal about the IPRC referral process through TDSB or TCDSB
tasks.tip: The IPRC process (including psycho-educational assessment) can take 2–3 months — start early to meet secondary school placement deadlines!

timeline.description: Attend information events and narrow down your options.

tasks.text: Attend your TDSB or TCDSB Grade 8 information night for high school options
tasks.tip: Both boards host annual information events for Grade 8 families. Check tdsb.on.ca or tcdsb.org for dates — they typically run October through November.

tasks.text: Visit the websites of 2–3 Toronto schools you're curious about
tasks.tip: Look for: program listings, clubs and sports, virtual tours, and student testimonial videos. Both TDSB and TCDSB school profiles are on their board websites.

tasks.text: Talk to Grade 9 students or older teens you know — ask what they wish they'd known
tasks.tip: Personal experience is often the most honest source of information about a school's culture.

tasks.text: Check TDSB and TCDSB application deadlines for specialized programs you're considering
tasks.tip: Most TDSB specialized program applications open in November and close in January. TCDSB deadlines are typically December–January. Dates vary by program.

tasks.text: If applying to arts programs, start working on or selecting portfolio pieces
tasks.tip: Visual arts portfolios typically need 3–6 pieces. Choose work that shows range and your best technical skill.

timeline.description: Attend Open Houses and begin preparing applications.

tasks.text: Attend Open Houses at TDSB and TCDSB schools you're considering (most are held November–January)
tasks.tip: Open Houses are your best chance to see the school, meet current students, and speak with teachers in specialized programs.

tasks.text: Submit applications for TDSB or TCDSB specialized programs that open in November
tasks.tip: TDSB applications are submitted through tdsb.on.ca. TCDSB applications through tcdsb.org. Some programs have school-specific forms — read carefully.

tasks.text: Ask a teacher for a reference letter if your application requires one
tasks.tip: Give teachers at least 2 weeks' notice. Share your interests and the specific Toronto program you're applying to so they can write specifically.

tasks.text: If auditioning for music/drama/dance at a TDSB or TCDSB arts school, begin preparing your audition material
tasks.tip: Most auditions require a 1–2 minute prepared piece. Practice in front of a mirror or record yourself.

tasks.text: Discuss your top Toronto school options with a parent or guardian
tasks.tip: Make sure everyone is aligned on TTC commute logistics, application requirements, and decision timelines.

timeline.description: Submit applications and confirm your choices before the winter break.

tasks.text: Submit any TDSB or TCDSB specialized program applications with December deadlines
tasks.tip: Don't wait until the last day — submit early and keep a copy of your confirmation email or application number.

tasks.text: If applying out of area, check when TDSB or TCDSB transfer applications open (usually January)
tasks.tip: Out-of-area transfer applications are separate from specialized program applications and are handled by your board's Student Placement office.

tasks.text: Finalize your portfolio or audition prep before the holiday break
tasks.tip: You'll have less time to prepare in January once school resumes.

tasks.text: Confirm your home school registration — verify your TDSB or TCDSB catchment address
tasks.tip: Even if you plan to attend your home school, confirm the registration process with your local TDSB or TCDSB school office.

tasks.text: Make a list of your top 2–3 Toronto school choices and rank them in order of preference
tasks.tip: Having a clear ranking helps you make decisions quickly when offers arrive in the spring.

timeline.description: Meet the main application deadline month — most TDSB and TCDSB programs close in January.

tasks.text: Submit remaining TDSB or TCDSB specialized program applications (most close January–February)
tasks.tip: This is the highest-stakes month. Double-check all requirements — TDSB at tdsb.on.ca, TCDSB at tcdsb.org — before submitting.

tasks.text: Submit out-of-area transfer application to TDSB or TCDSB (if applicable)
tasks.tip: TDSB: contact your local school or Student Support Services. TCDSB: contact the Student Placement office at tcdsb.org. Include a specific, clear reason for your request.

tasks.text: Attend any remaining TDSB or TCDSB school Open Houses
tasks.tip: Some Toronto schools host Open Houses in January as a final chance before applications close.

tasks.text: Follow up with teachers who wrote reference letters to confirm they submitted
tasks.tip: A polite thank-you note and a check-in goes a long way.

tasks.text: Confirm IPRC assessment appointment through TDSB or TCDSB (if pursuing gifted identification)
tasks.tip: If you haven't been contacted yet, follow up with your school's Special Education resource teacher or contact your board's Special Education department directly.

timeline.description: Auditions, assessments, and waiting for decisions.

tasks.text: Attend scheduled auditions or assessments for TDSB or TCDSB specialized programs
tasks.tip: Arrive early, bring everything you need, and remember: they're assessing potential, not just current skill.

tasks.text: Follow up on out-of-area transfer status with TDSB or TCDSB if you haven't heard back
tasks.tip: A polite email to your board's Student Placement office is appropriate if you haven't received a response within 4–6 weeks.

tasks.text: Complete any IB academic assessments or interviews if scheduled
tasks.tip: TDSB and TCDSB IB programs may invite shortlisted students for a conversation about their goals and study habits.

tasks.text: Keep your Grade 8 marks strong — TDSB and TCDSB programs may check your final Grade 8 marks
tasks.tip: Even after submitting applications, your academic performance still matters for final decisions.

tasks.text: Talk to your school counselor if you're feeling anxious about the process
tasks.tip: It's completely normal to feel uncertain. Remember: all four options lead to a great Toronto high school experience.

timeline.description: Receive offers and make your final decision.

tasks.text: Open your TDSB or TCDSB offer letters or acceptance notifications when they arrive
tasks.tip: Decisions are typically sent by email or through the application portal. Check your inbox and spam folder.

tasks.text: If accepted to a specialized program, confirm your acceptance by the deadline
tasks.tip: Most TDSB and TCDSB programs give you 1–2 weeks to accept. Miss the deadline and your spot may go to the waitlist.

tasks.text: If waitlisted, contact the school to confirm your status and timeline
tasks.tip: Spots do open up. Stay polite and express continued interest to the school's main office.

tasks.text: If rejected, finalize your plan to attend your TDSB or TCDSB home school or out-of-area choice
tasks.tip: A rejection from a specialized program doesn't define you — many of Toronto's most successful people attended their neighbourhood school.

tasks.text: Make your final school decision and notify your elementary school principal
tasks.tip: Your elementary school submits Grade 8 records to your receiving high school through the TDSB or TCDSB — they need to know where you're going.

tasks.text: Register at your chosen Toronto high school (if not done automatically through TDSB or TCDSB)
tasks.tip: Some schools host Grade 9 registration days in May or June. Bring your report card and any required documents (e.g., baptismal certificate for TCDSB).

tasks.text: Attend Grade 9 orientation day at your Toronto high school
tasks.tip: Orientation days help you meet your new peers, learn the building layout, and start to feel at home.

tasks.text: Review your Grade 9 course selection form with a parent or guardian
tasks.tip: Your course choices in Grade 9 matter! Ask the school's guidance counselor if you have questions about destination codes (U, C, E, O).

tasks.text: Connect with incoming Grade 9 students through school social media or orientation events
tasks.tip: Many Toronto high schools have Instagram accounts or Discord servers for incoming students to meet before September.

tasks.text: Celebrate! You navigated one of the most important decisions of your school life
tasks.tip: Whatever Toronto school you're attending — high school is a fresh start. Make the most of it.
